module-5-gradebook-project.label: Module Project: Build a Gradebook
module-5-gradebook-project.summary: A teacher asks you to build a small gradebook so names, scores, and class summaries stop living on sticky notes.

sketch0.title: Your first client: a teacher with messy scores

mp-1-create-student-list.title: Create a student list
mp-1-create-student-list.prompt: Ms. Rivera is setting up her class roster. Create an empty list named `students`, add `Ava`, `Mia`, and `Zoe` in that order, then print the list so she can confirm the roster starts correctly.
mp-1-create-student-list.hint: The roster should grow one name at a time. Use `append()` for each student, then print the finished list.

help.concept: This project grows like a real gradebook: each step starts from a clean working version, then adds one useful feature for the teacher.
help.hint_1: Start with `students = []`, then append the three names in order.
help.hint_2: The teacher needs to see the roster, so the final line should print `students`.

mp-1-create-student-list.starterCode:
students = []

# Add Ava, Mia, and Zoe to the list in that order.
# Then print the list.

mp-2-store-scores.title: Store scores in a dictionary
mp-2-store-scores.prompt: Now Ms. Rivera has scores for the same three students. Keep the completed `students` list, create a dictionary named `scores` with `Ava: 92`, `Mia: 85`, and `Zoe: 97`, then print both the roster and the score dictionary.
mp-2-store-scores.hint: Use the names as dictionary keys and the numbers as values.

help.concept: A list keeps the class roster in order; a dictionary lets the gradebook look up each student’s score by name.
help.hint_1: Create `scores = {"Ava": 92, "Mia": 85, "Zoe": 97}` after the student list is built.
help.hint_2: Print `students` first, then print `scores` so both parts of the gradebook are visible.

mp-2-store-scores.starterCode:
students = []
students.append('Ava')
students.append('Mia')
students.append('Zoe')
print(students)

# Now create a dictionary named scores for the same students.
# Ava has 92, Mia has 85, and Zoe has 97.
# Print the dictionary.

mp-3-print-passing-students.title: Print high-scoring students
mp-3-print-passing-students.prompt: Ms. Rivera wants a quick list of students ready for the honor board. Starting from the roster and score dictionary, print only the students whose score is 90 or higher, one name per line.
mp-3-print-passing-students.hint: Loop through the roster and use the score dictionary to decide who should be printed.

help.concept: This step connects two collections: the list gives the order, and the dictionary gives the score for each name.
help.hint_1: Use `for student in students:` to visit names in roster order.
help.hint_2: Inside the loop, check `scores[student] >= 90` before printing the name.

mp-3-print-passing-students.starterCode:
students = ['Ava', 'Mia', 'Zoe']
scores = {'Ava': 92, 'Mia': 85, 'Zoe': 97}
print(students)
print(scores)

# Print only students with scores of 90 or higher, one per line.

mp-4-sort-grade-summary.title: Sort the grade summary
mp-4-sort-grade-summary.prompt: For the final gradebook view, Ms. Rivera wants the strongest scores at the top. Start from the completed gradebook, sort the score summary from highest score to lowest score, and print each line as `Name: score`.
mp-4-sort-grade-summary.hint: Sort the dictionary items by score in reverse order, then format each line.

help.concept: A useful report is not just stored data; it presents the data in an order that helps someone make a decision.
help.hint_2: Loop through the sorted pairs and print each line with an f-string like `f"{name}: {score}"`.

try-module-5-gradebook-project-sketch0.title: Preview a gradebook list
try-module-5-gradebook-project-sketch0.prompt: Use `append()` to preview how the gradebook project will grow a student list. Finish the small program, run it, and check that the result fits the scenario.
try-module-5-gradebook-project-sketch0.hint: Finish the small program in `main.py`, then run it to check that it behaves like the task asks.

help.concept: This Try it yourself checkpoint turns the lesson idea from "Build a gradebook step by step" with a small runnable program.
help.hint_1: Use the TODO comments as your checklist for this small job.
help.hint_2: Make sure the final program shows the result the scenario needs.

try-module-5-gradebook-project-sketch0.starterCode:
students = ['Ava', 'Mia']

# Add Zoe and print students.

try_module_5_gradebook_project_sketch0.title: Try it yourself
try_module_5_gradebook_project_sketch0.prompt: Use `append()` to preview how the gradebook project will grow a student list.

sketch-1.title: Your first client: a teacher with messy scores
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
Ms. Rivera has student names in one place, scores in another, and no clean way to see who is doing well. In this module project, you will build a small gradebook for her.

The project grows smoothly: each step starts from the previous canonical solution and adds one new feature. By the end, the program will keep a roster, connect names to scores, filter high-scoring students, and print a sorted summary.
